file_name: Non Disclosure Agreement - CP Specific NDA (CreativeNDA-CPS121.1014).txt

perturbation.original_text: “Purpose”: means th e exchange of C onfidential I nformation, t rade secrets, know -how and samples in order t o evaluat e and pursu e the objectiv e of one or mor e potential business arrangements with respect to CP’s potential supply the Company of pultruded materials of designs that are proprietary to the Company ____________________________________________________________________.
perturbation.changed_text: “Purpose”: means th e exchange of C onfidential I nformation, t rade secrets, know -how and samples in order t o evaluat e and pursu e the objectiv e of one or mor e potential business arrangements with respect to CP’s potential supply the Company of pultruded materials of designs that are proprietary to CP ____________________________________________________________________.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines the Purpose as CP supplying the Company with pultruded materials of designs proprietary to the *Company*. The modification changes this to designs proprietary to *CP*. This directly contradicts Section 5.1, where the Disclosing Party warrants it has the right to disclose its Confidential Information, which now includes designs that are *not* proprietary to them, but to CP, creating uncertainty about the scope of permitted disclosure and use. The definition of "Purpose" is a key term since it defines the extent of what both parties wish to acheive, and with this changed definition, it introduces a new contradiction within the text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Disc losing Party warrants that it has the right to disclose its Confidential Information to the Recipient and to authorise the Recipient to use such Confidential Information for the Purpose.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Purpose' to include CP's proprietary designs, directly contradicting the 'Contradicted Text', where the Disclosing Party warrants it has the right to disclose its Confidential Information. Since the Purpose now involves CP's designs, the Company (if it is the disclosing party) would be warranting it can disclose information that is proprietary to CP, which it wouldn't have the right to do, thus creating a direct conflict and an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Inf ormation:” means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representa tives including but not limited to: the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; any inform ation that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (a) the business, affai rs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; (b) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specificatio ns, trade secrets or so ftware of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; and (c) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Inform ation; but not including any info rmation: 1 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 (a) that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the Party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless betreated as Confidential Information); (b) was available to the Recipient on a non- confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) was, is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non-confidential basis from a person who, to the Recipient's knowledge, is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Partyor otherwise prohibited from disclosing the information to the Recipient; (d) was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; (e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed; and/or (f) is developed by or for the Recipient independently of the information disclosed by theDisclosing Pa rty.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Inf ormation:” means only that confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representa tives including but not limited to: the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; any inform ation that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (a) the business, affai rs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; (b) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specificatio ns, trade secrets or so ftware of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; and (c) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Inform ation; but not including any info rmation: 1 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 (a) that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the Party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless betreated as Confidential Information); (b) was available to the Recipient on a non- confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) was, is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non-confidential basis from a person who, to the Recipient's knowledge, is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Partyor otherwise prohibited from disclosing the information to the Recipient; (d) was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; (e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed; and/or (f) is developed by or for the Recipient independently of the information disclosed by theDisclosing Pa rty.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "all confidential information" to "only that confidential information", the definition of 'Confidential Information' is severely restricted. However, section 2.1 (a) still states that the Recipient shall not use or exploit "the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose". Because of that change, it is contradictory, now, that the confidentiality clause applies only to *that* confidential information, yet the clauses that refer to it are still acting like *all* confidential information is included. This contradiction in scope creates uncertainty regarding the actual obligations of the Recipient.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipi ent shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Part y, shall: (a) not use or ex ploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this A greement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictlynecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be theproperty of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of theRecipient; (e) apply the same security measures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as theRecipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providingadequate p rotection from unauthorised disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the otherin tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly. The changed text restricts the definition by using 'only that confidential information', which directly contradicts the contradicted text in section (a). Section (a) states that the Recipient shall not use or exploit 'the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose'. The change in the definition now means that only *that* information is restricted. If the Recipient exploits information that isn't strictly defined as 'that confidential information' they would be within compliance of the agreement, which is not correct.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 The Recipi ent shall establish and maintain adequate security measures (including any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 The Recipi ent shall establish and maintain minimal security measures to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires "adequate security measures," which implies a reasonable and effective level of protection. The modification changes this to "minimal security measures," which suggests the *lowest possible level of security*. This directly contradicts the intent of the agreement to protect sensitive information, creating a legal loophole. Section 2.1(e) states that the Recipient should provide adequate protection from unauthorised disclosure, so if section 2.5 states minimal security measures, and section 2.1(e) states that adequate security measures are to be used. This introduces a clear internal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipi ent shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Part y, shall: (a) not use or ex ploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this A greement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictlynecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be theproperty of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of theRecipient; (e) apply the same security measures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as theRecipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providingadequate p rotection from unauthorised disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the otherin tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates "adequate security measures," while the changed text mandates "minimal security measures." This directly contradicts section 2.1(e), which requires the Recipient to apply security measures that provide "adequate protection from unauthorised disclosure." 'Minimal' and 'adequate' are contradictory terms in this context, creating a clear and strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Each Recipi ent shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party and its Group at all times against all liabilities, costs (includ ing legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group) a rising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Each Recipi ent shall indemnify the Disclosing Party for reasonable and direct costs only that arose from a breach of this agreement, and not for indirect or consequential damages.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the Recipient to *fully indemnify* the Disclosing Party against *all* liabilities, including direct, indirect, and consequential losses. The modified version limits this to indemnification only for "reasonable and direct costs," explicitly excluding indirect or consequential damages. This creates a substantial contradiction, as the scope of indemnification is drastically reduced. Section 4.4 says damages alone would not be an adequate remedy, and now section 5.2 is stating that only direct costs would be covered, in contradiction with the earlier statement that damages alone would not be enough.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipi ent acknowledges that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement. Accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this A greement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 5.2 required the Recipient to fully indemnify the Disclosing Party against all liabilities, including direct, indirect, and consequential losses. The changed text in Section 5.2 limits indemnification to reasonable and direct costs only, excluding indirect or consequential damages. This contradicts the statement in the Contradicted Text, which says that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for a breach of the agreement. The modified clause reduces the scope of indemnification, conflicting with the understanding that damages alone are insufficient.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The obliga tions of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiati ons or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 2 years from the date of this A greement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiati ons or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 2 years, unless otherwise agreed upon by the parties involved.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that obligations continue for a period of 2 years. The modified version now adds "unless otherwise agreed upon by the parties involved." This introduces a contradiction as it makes the original term duration uncertain and unenforceable. Section 7.1 states this agreement constitutes the entire agreement between parties. And now with this new text, it allows for a easy workaround, which contradicts the earlier statement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreemen t constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties and supersedes and extinguishes all previous drafts, agreements, arrangements and understandings between them,whether written or oral, relating to its subject matter.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the obligations continue for 2 years. The changed text introduces the phrase "unless otherwise agreed upon by the parties involved." This creates a strong contradiction with the contradicted text, which states that the agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior agreements. The changed text now allows for other agreements to modify the 2-year obligation, directly contradicting the entire agreement clause. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction, making the original term duration uncertain and unenforceable. This new text allows for an easy workaround, which contradicts the earlier statement.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This A greement and any di spute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof. The P arties irrevocably agree that the C ourts o f Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non- contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This A greement and any di spute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof. The P arties agree that the C ourts o f Delaware shall have jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non- contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies that the parties *irrevocably agree* that the Courts of Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction. The modified version removes the word "irrevocably" and changes "exclusive jurisdiction" to just "jurisdiction". This subtle change creates a major contradiction, because section 7.2 states that the parties agree to have no remedies in respect to representation or warrantly that is not set out in the agreement. This would have to take place in a Delaware Court, and section 16, now allows for the possibility for another court to be in charge. And if that is the case, then remedies might not be available to the parties, therefore creating a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party agrees that it shall have no remedies in respect of any representation or warranty (whether made innocently or negligently) that is not set out in this Agreement. Each Party agrees that its only liability in respect of those representations and warranties that are set out in this Agreement (whether made innocently or negligently) shall be for breach of contract.
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 16 removes the 'irrevocably' and changes 'exclusive jurisdiction' to 'jurisdiction'. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text which states that each party agrees they have no remedies in respect to any representation or warranty that is not set out in the agreement. The removal of 'irrevocably' and 'exclusive jurisdiction' allows for disputes to potentially be settled outside of Delaware courts, which could affect the remedies available to the parties, contradicting the earlier clause. The explanation states that 'section 7.2 states that the parties agree to have no remedies in respect to representation or warrantly that is not set out in the agreement. This would have to take place in a Delaware Court, and section 16, now allows for the possibility for another court to be in charge. And if that is the case, then remedies might not be available to the parties, therefore creating a contradiction.'
